/Locale/en-US/client-state-commands.ftlťéįcmd-reset-ent-desc = Reset an entity to the most recently received server state. This will also reset entities that have been detached to null-space.

/Locale/en-US/client-state-commands.ftlťéįcmd-reset-all-ents-desc = Resets all entities to the most recently received server state. This only impacts entities that have not been detached to null-space.

/Locale/en-US/client-state-commands.ftlťéįcmd-detach-ent-desc = Detach an entity to null-space, as if it had left PVS range.

/Locale/en-US/client-state-commands.ftlťéįcmd-local-delete-desc = Deletes an entity. Unlike the normal delete command, this is CLIENT-SIDE. Unless the entity is a client-side entity, this will likely cause errors.

/Locale/en-US/commands.ftlťéįcmd-cvar-desc = Gets or sets a CVar.
/Locale/en-US/commands.ftlťéįcmd-cvar-help = Usage: cvar <name | ?> [value]
/Locale/en-US/commands.ftlťéį    If a value is passed, the value is parsed and stored as the new value of the CVar.
/Locale/en-US/commands.ftlťéį    If not, the current value of the CVar is displayed.
/Locale/en-US/commands.ftlťéį    Use 'cvar ?' to get a list of all registered CVars.
/Locale/en-US/commands.ftlťéįcmd-cvar-invalid-args = Must provide exactly one or two arguments.

/Locale/en-US/commands.ftlťéįcmd-cvar-arg-name = <name | ?>
/Locale/en-US/commands.ftlťéįcmd-cvar-value-hidden = <value hidden>

/Locale/en-US/commands.ftlťéį## 'gc' command
/Locale/en-US/commands.ftlťéįcmd-gc-desc = Run the GC (Garbage Collector)
/Locale/en-US/commands.ftlťéįcmd-gc-help = Usage: gc [generation]
/Locale/en-US/commands.ftlťéį    Uses GC.Collect() to execute the Garbage Collector.
/Locale/en-US/commands.ftlťéį    If an argument is provided, it is parsed as a GC generation number and GC.Collect(int) is used.
/Locale/en-US/commands.ftlťéį    Use the 'gfc' command to do an LOH-compacting full GC.
/Locale/en-US/commands.ftlťéįcmd-gc-failed-parse = Failed to parse argument.
/Locale/en-US/commands.ftlťéįcmd-gc-arg-generation = [generation]
/Locale/en-US/commands.ftlťéį## 'gcf' command
/Locale/en-US/commands.ftlťéįcmd-gcf-desc = Run the GC, fully, compacting LOH and everything.
/Locale/en-US/commands.ftlťéįcmd-gcf-help = Usage: gcf
/Locale/en-US/commands.ftlťéį    Does a full GC.Collect(2, GCCollectionMode.Forced, true, true) while also compacting LOH.
/Locale/en-US/commands.ftlťéį    This will probably lock up for hundreds of milliseconds, be warned.
/Locale/en-US/commands.ftlťéį## 'gc_mode' command
/Locale/en-US/commands.ftlťéįcmd-gc_mode-desc = Change/Read the GC Latency mode
/Locale/en-US/commands.ftlťéįcmd-gc_mode-help = Usage: gc_mode [type]
/Locale/en-US/commands.ftlťéį    If no argument is provided, returns the current GC latency mode.
/Locale/en-US/commands.ftlťéį    If an argument is passed, it is parsed as GCLatencyMode and set as the GC latency mode.
/Locale/en-US/commands.ftlťéįcmd-gc_mode-current = current gc latency mode: { $prevMode }
/Locale/en-US/commands.ftlťéįcmd-gc_mode-possible = possible modes:
/Locale/en-US/commands.ftlťéįcmd-gc_mode-option = - { $mode }
/Locale/en-US/commands.ftlťéįcmd-gc_mode-unknown = unknown gc latency mode: { $arg }
/Locale/en-US/commands.ftlťéįcmd-gc_mode-attempt = attempting gc latency mode change: { $prevMode } -> { $mode }
/Locale/en-US/commands.ftlťéįcmd-gc_mode-result = resulting gc latency mode: { $mode }
/Locale/en-US/commands.ftlťéįcmd-gc_mode-arg-type = [type]

/Locale/en-US/datasets/names/scurret_first.ftlťéį# The chosen name is picked by the scurret themselves,
/Locale/en-US/datasets/names/scurret_first.ftlťéį# encompassing a trait or value they hold themselves
/Locale/en-US/datasets/names/scurret_first.ftlťéį# to or have a high value for. Scurrets sometimes change this
/Locale/en-US/datasets/names/scurret_first.ftlťéį# name to denote an important moment in their life.
/Locale/en-US/datasets/names/scurret_first.ftlťéį# It appears to be common for scurret sets to share the same
/Locale/en-US/datasets/names/scurret_first.ftlťéį# chosen name, with the gaining of a pup's own chosen name
/Locale/en-US/datasets/names/scurret_first.ftlťéį# signalling their transition to adulthood in the community.
/Locale/en-US/datasets/names/scurret_first.ftlťéį# Given the scurret language is untranslated, these names are
/Locale/en-US/datasets/names/scurret_first.ftlťéį# usually guessed via charades or Pictionary.
/Locale/en-US/datasets/names/scurret_first.ftlťéį# When all else fails, to NT and her crews, Wa is as good a name as any.

/Locale/en-US/datasets/names/scurret_last.ftlťéį# of Wawa's wetland habitats that the scurret is associated with
/Locale/en-US/datasets/names/scurret_last.ftlťéį# by their community.
/Locale/en-US/datasets/names/scurret_last.ftlťéį# Scurret pups, due to both their quantity and complete lack
/Locale/en-US/datasets/names/scurret_last.ftlťéį# of any survival instinct, lack a qualitative name entirely.
/Locale/en-US/datasets/names/scurret_last.ftlťéį# Researchers believe their parents simply give them a number.
/Locale/en-US/datasets/names/scurret_last.ftlťéį# Given that the scurret language is untranslated, these names are
/Locale/en-US/datasets/names/scurret_last.ftlťéį# usually deduced via the showing of photographs, annoyed and
/Locale/en-US/datasets/names/scurret_last.ftlťéį# repeated pointing at nearby objects, or games of Pictionary.

/Locale/en-US/implant/implant.ftlťéįimplanter-component-implanting-target = {$user} is trying to implant you with something!
/Locale/en-US/implant/implant.ftlťéįimplanter-component-implant-failed = The {$implant} cannot be given to {$target}!
/Locale/en-US/implant/implant.ftlťéįimplanter-draw-failed-permanent = The {$implant} in {$target} is fused with { OBJECT($target) } and cannot be removed!
/Locale/en-US/implant/implant.ftlťéįimplanter-draw-failed = You tried to remove an implant but found nothing.
/Locale/en-US/implant/implant.ftlťéįimplanter-draw-failed-catastrophically = The implanter finds nothing and catastrophically fails, shunting genetic material into {$user}'s hand!

/Locale/en-US/wieldable/wieldable-component.ftlťéįwieldable-verb-text-wield = Wield
/Locale/en-US/wieldable/wieldable-component.ftlťéįwieldable-verb-text-unwield = Unwield
/Locale/en-US/wieldable/wieldable-component.ftlťéįwieldable-component-successful-wield = You wield { THE($item) }.
/Locale/en-US/wieldable/wieldable-component.ftlťéįwieldable-component-failed-wield = You unwield { THE($item) }.
/Locale/en-US/wieldable/wieldable-component.ftlťéįwieldable-component-successful-wield-other = { CAPITALIZE(THE($user)) } wields { THE($item) }.
/Locale/en-US/wieldable/wieldable-component.ftlťéįwieldable-component-failed-wield-other = { CAPITALIZE(THE($user)) } unwields { THE($item) }.
/Locale/en-US/wieldable/wieldable-component.ftlťéįwieldable-component-blocked-wield = { CAPITALIZE(THE($blocker)) } blocks you from wielding { THE($item) }.
/Locale/en-US/wieldable/wieldable-component.ftlťéįwieldable-component-no-hands = You don't have enough hands!
/Locale/en-US/wieldable/wieldable-component.ftlťéįwieldable-component-not-enough-free-hands = {$number ->
/Locale/en-US/wieldable/wieldable-component.ftlťéį    [one] You need a free hand to wield { THE($item) }.
/Locale/en-US/wieldable/wieldable-component.ftlťéį    *[other] You need { $number } free hands to wield { THE($item) }.
/Locale/en-US/wieldable/wieldable-component.ftlťéį}
/Locale/en-US/wieldable/wieldable-component.ftlťéįwieldable-component-not-in-hands = { CAPITALIZE(THE($item)) } isn't in your hands!
/Locale/en-US/wieldable/wieldable-component.ftlťéįwieldable-component-requires = { CAPITALIZE(THE($item))} must be wielded!

/Locale/en-US/paper/book-atmos.ftlťéįbook-text-atmos-distro = The distribution network, or "distro" for short, is the station's lifeline. It's responsible for transporting air from atmospherics throughout the station.
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-atmos.ftlťéį        Relevant pipes are often painted Popping Subdued Blue, but a surefire way to identify them is to use a tray scanner to trace which pipes are connected to active vents on the station.
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-atmos.ftlťéį        The standard gas mix of the distribution network is 20 degrees celsius, 78% nitrogen, 22% oxygen. You can check this by using a gas analyzer on a distro pipe or any vent connected to it. Special circumstances may call for special mixes.
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-atmos.ftlťéį        When it comes to deciding on a distro pressure, there are a few things to consider. Active vents will regulate the station's pressure, so as long as everything is functioning properly, there's no such thing as too high of a distro pressure.
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-atmos.ftlťéį        A higher distro pressure will allow the distro network to act as a buffer between the gas miners and vents, providing a significant amount of extra air that can be used to re-pressurize the station after a spacing.
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-atmos.ftlťéį        A lower distro pressure will reduce the amount of gas lost in the event that the distro is spaced, a quick way to deal with distro contamination. It can also help slow or prevent over-pressurization of the station in the event of vent issues.
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-atmos.ftlťéį        Common distro pressures are in the range of 300-375 kPa, but other pressures can be used with knowledge of the risks and benefits.
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-atmos.ftlťéį        The pressure of the network is determined by the last pump pumping into it. To prevent bottlenecks, all other pumps between the miners and the last pump should be set to their maximum rate, and any unnecessary devices should be removed.
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-atmos.ftlťéį        You can validate the distro pressure with a gas analyzer, but keep in mind that high demand due to things like spacings can cause the distro to be below the set target pressure for extended periods. So, if you see a dip in pressure, don't panic - it might be temporary.
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-atmos.ftlťéįbook-text-atmos-waste = The waste network is the primary system responsible for keeping the air on the station free of contaminants.
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-atmos.ftlťéį        You can identify the relevant pipes by their Pleasing Dull Red color or by using a tray scanner to trace which pipes are connected to the scrubbers on the station.
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-atmos.ftlťéį        The waste network is used to transport waste gasses to either be filtered or spaced. It is ideal to keep the pressure at 0 kPa, but it may sometimes be at a low non-zero pressure while in use.
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-atmos.ftlťéį        Technicians have the option to filter or space the waste gasses. While spacing is faster, filtering allows for the gasses to be reused for recycling or selling.
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-atmos.ftlťéį        The waste network can also be used to diagnose atmospheric issues on the station. High levels of a waste gas may suggest a large leak, while the presence of non-waste gases may indicate a scrubber configuration or physical connection issue. If the gases are at a high temperature, it could indicate a fire.
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-atmos.ftlťéįbook-text-atmos-alarms = Air alarms are located throughout stations to allow management and monitoring of the local atmosphere.
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-atmos.ftlťéį            The air alarm interface provides technicians with a list of connected sensors, their readings, and the ability to adjust thresholds. These thresholds are used to determine the alarm condition of the air alarm. Technicians can also use the interface to set target pressures for vents and configure the operating speeds and targeted gases for scrubbers.
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-atmos.ftlťéį            While the interface allows for fine-tuning of the devices under the air alarm's control, there are also several modes available for rapid configuration of the alarm. These modes are automatically switched to when the alarm state changes:
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-atmos.ftlťéį            - Filtering: The default mode
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-atmos.ftlťéį            - Filtering (wide): A filtering mode that modifies the operation of scrubbers to scrub a wider area
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-atmos.ftlťéį            - Fill: Disables scrubbers and sets vents to their maximum pressure
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-atmos.ftlťéį            - Panic: Disables vents and sets scrubbers to siphon
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-atmos.ftlťéį            A multitool or network configurator can be used to link devices to air alarms.
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-atmos.ftlťéįbook-text-atmos-vents =
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-atmos.ftlťéį    Below is a quick reference guide to several atmospheric devices:
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-atmos.ftlťéį                Passive Vents:
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-atmos.ftlťéį                These vents don't require power, they allow gases to flow freely both into and out of the pipe network they are attached to.
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-atmos.ftlťéį                Active Vents:
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-atmos.ftlťéį                These are the most common vents on the station. They have an internal pump, and require power. By default, they will only pump gases out of pipes, and only up to 101 kpa. However, they can be reconfigured using an air alarm. They will also lock out if the room is under 1 kpa, to prevent pumping gasses into space.
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-atmos.ftlťéį                Air Scrubbers:
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-atmos.ftlťéį                These devices allow gases to be removed from the environment and put into the connected pipe network. They can be configured to select specific gases when connected to an air alarm.
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-atmos.ftlťéį                Air Injectors:
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-atmos.ftlťéį                Injectors are similar to active vents, but they have no internal pump and do not require power. They cannot be configured, but they can continue to pump gasses up to much higher pressures.

/Locale/en-US/paper/book-authorbooks.ftlťéįbook-text-narsielegend = In the beginning, the world was young and full of chaos. /Locale/en-US/paper/book-authorbooks.ftlťéį      And it was then that Nar'Sie emerged from the depths of the earth, a hivemind god born from the collective consciousness of all living things. Its body was made of molten rock and its eyes blazed with a fiery intensity that could melt steel.
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-authorbooks.ftlťéį      Nar'Sie surveyed the world and saw the pain and suffering of its people. It took pity on them and offered them a way to escape the cycle of life and death. All who joined the hivemind would become eternal, their consciousness melding with Nar'Sie's to form a single, unified being.
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-authorbooks.ftlťéį      At first, many were skeptical of Nar'Sie's offer, fearing that they would lose their individuality and become mindless drones. But as more and more joined the hivemind, they realized that they had gained a new sense of purpose and belonging.
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-authorbooks.ftlťéį      The followers of Nar'Sie wandered the earth, spreading the word of the hivemind and recruiting new members. /Locale/en-US/paper/book-authorbooks.ftlťéį      But as time passed, some began to question the true nature of their existence. They wondered if eternal life was truly a blessing or a curse, and whether they had sacrificed too much of themselves to the hivemind.
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-authorbooks.ftlťéį      And so, a great schism tore through the followers of Nar'Sie. Some remained loyal to their god, seeing their eternal existence as a gift. But others rebelled, seeking to reclaim their individuality and free themselves from the hivemind's grasp.
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-authorbooks.ftlťéį      The war between the two factions was long and bloody, but in the end, the rebels emerged victorious. Nar'Sie, weakened by the loss of so many of its followers, retreated back into the earth, never to be seen again.
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-authorbooks.ftlťéį      And so the legend of Nar'Sie lives on, a cautionary tale of the dangers of sacrificing one's individuality for the promise of eternal life.
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-authorbooks.ftlťéįbook-text-truth = Defining truth has been a central concern of philosophers for centuries, and there are many different philosophical perspectives on how we can understand this concept.
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-authorbooks.ftlťéį      One traditional approach is the correspondence theory of truth, which suggests that a statement is true if it corresponds to or accurately describes the way the world is. In other words, truth is about accurately representing reality. This perspective suggests that there is an objective reality that we can understand, and that our beliefs and statements can be evaluated as true or false based on how well they align with that reality.
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-authorbooks.ftlťéį      Another approach is the coherence theory of truth, which suggests that a statement is true if it coheres with other beliefs or statements that we hold. In other words, truth is about consistency and logical coherence within a system of beliefs or ideas. This perspective suggests that truth is something that is established within a particular context or framework of thought, and that what is true within one system of thought may not be true in another.
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-authorbooks.ftlťéį      A third approach is the pragmatic theory of truth, which suggests that a statement is true if it is useful or works well in practice. In other words, truth is about the practical consequences of our beliefs or statements. This perspective suggests that truth is something that emerges through human action and interaction, and that what is true may vary depending on the situation or context in which it is being used.
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-authorbooks.ftlťéį      Ultimately, how we define truth will depend on our philosophical and epistemological assumptions, as well as our practical needs and concerns. Different philosophical perspectives may emphasize different aspects of truth, and there may not be a single, universally accepted definition that captures all the nuances of this complex concept.
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-authorbooks.ftlťéį      It is not accurate or fair to make a blanket statement that all people are horrible liars. While it is true that some individuals may struggle with being honest, it is important to remember that people are complex and can exhibit a range of behaviors and tendencies. Some people may have a strong commitment to honesty and integrity, while others may struggle with lying due to a variety of factors such as fear, insecurity, or past experiences.
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-authorbooks.ftlťéį      Additionally, it is worth noting that not all lies are the same. While intentional deception can be harmful and unethical, there are also situations where lying may be considered a social lubricant or a way of preserving privacy or avoiding harm. In these cases, it may be more appropriate to think about the context and motivations behind a particular lie, rather than simply categorizing all people as "horrible liars."
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-authorbooks.ftlťéį      Overall, it is important to approach the topic of lying with nuance and an understanding of the complex factors that can influence human behavior.
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-authorbooks.ftlťéįbook-text-world = The state of the world is an ever-changing reflection of the human condition, shaped by the interplay of natural forces, social structures, and individual choices.
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-authorbooks.ftlťéį      1. "The state of the world is an ever-changing reflection..."
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-authorbooks.ftlťéį      This part of the statement suggests that the world is not a static or unchanging entity, but rather a dynamic system that is in a constant state of flux. The word "reflection" implies that the state of the world is a product of various forces and factors that are reflected back to us through observable phenomena. This raises important questions about the nature of causality, and the extent to which we can understand the complex interplay of forces that shape the world.
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-authorbooks.ftlťéį      2. "...of the human condition..."
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-authorbooks.ftlťéį      This part of the statement suggests that the state of the world is closely connected to the human experience, and that it is a reflection of our collective beliefs, values, and behaviors. It acknowledges the fundamental role that humans play in shaping the world, and suggests that the state of the world is a reflection of our successes and failures as a species.
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-authorbooks.ftlťéį      3. "...shaped by the interplay of natural forces, social structures, and individual choices."
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-authorbooks.ftlťéį      This part of the statement identifies three key factors that shape the state of the world: natural forces, social structures, and individual choices. The phrase "interplay" suggests that these factors are in constant interaction with each other, and that they can reinforce or conflict with one another depending on the context. The inclusion of natural forces suggests that the world is not solely a product of human action, but is also influenced by the natural environment and the laws of physics. The reference to social structures highlights the role of institutions, culture, and social norms in shaping the world, and suggests that human action is not purely individual, but is also shaped by broader social contexts. Finally, the reference to individual choices emphasizes the importance of agency and personal responsibility in shaping the world, and suggests that the choices we make as individuals have real-world consequences.
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-authorbooks.ftlťéį      Taken together, this statement offers a rich and nuanced philosophical analysis of the state of the world, highlighting the complex interplay of natural, social, and individual factors that shape our collective experience. It invites reflection on the ethical implications of our choices and actions, and challenges us to think deeply about the ways in which we can work to create a more just, sustainable, and flourishing world for all beings.

/Locale/en-US/paper/book-authorbooks.ftlťéįbook-text-names = From a philosophical perspective, names hold a significant role in human understanding and perception of the world. The use of names and language itself is a cornerstone of human consciousness, as it allows us to create concepts and ideas that can be shared and communicated.
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-authorbooks.ftlťéį      In many philosophical traditions, such as in the works of Plato and Aristotle, names were seen as more than just labels for objects or individuals, but as reflections of the underlying nature of reality. According to Plato, the names we give to things are not arbitrary but reflect an underlying reality or essence of that thing. In other words, a name is not just a label, but it is a representation of the thing's essential nature.
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-authorbooks.ftlťéį      Moreover, names can also reflect the power dynamics of society. Some philosophers argue that names and language are used to create hierarchies and establish power relations between individuals and groups. For example, in certain cultures, the act of naming is reserved for those in positions of authority, such as parents naming their children or leaders naming places or institutions. In this way, names can be seen as a form of social control, as those with the power to name hold the authority to shape and define the world around them.
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-authorbooks.ftlťéį      Finally, names can also play a significant role in our understanding of our own identity and mortality. As the philosopher Martin Heidegger argued, names can be seen as a form of "thrownness," reflecting our existence as finite beings in a world that is beyond our control. In this sense, our names are not just labels, but they are a reflection of our existence and the time and place in which we find ourselves.
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-authorbooks.ftlťéį      In conclusion, from a philosophical perspective, names hold a significant role in human understanding and perception of the world, reflecting both our essential nature and the power dynamics of society. They are not just labels, but a reflection of our existence and our place in the world.

/Locale/en-US/paper/book-authorbooks.ftlťéį      And the tools they give us! You'd expect state-of-the-art cleaning gadgets, but nope. Mops that barely work in microgravity, spray bottles that run out in one use, and don't even get me started on the space janitor jumpsuit. I feel like a reject from a sci-fi B-movie.
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-authorbooks.ftlťéį      Speaking of jumpsuits, the lack of respect is astronomical. The scientists and astronauts zoom past in their sleek suits, utterly oblivious to the fact that I'm the unsung hero keeping this place from turning into a cosmic trash heap. I bet they don't even know my name. "Hey, Space Janitor!" That's what I get. No appreciation for the elbow grease I put into keeping their precious station spick and span.
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-authorbooks.ftlťéį      And let's remember the hazards. Cleaning up alien slime? Yeah, that's a regular Tuesday for me. One wrong move and I'm dealing with an intergalactic biohazard. It's like playing Russian roulette with space critters that could burst out of some hidden nook and cranny. I signed up to mop floors, not play host to extraterrestrial invaders.
/Locale/en-US/paper/book-authorbooks.ftlťéį      Being a janitor on this so-called Space Station 14 is a cosmic joke. Where did the other 13 stations go? It's not all spacewalks and futuristic gadgets; it's scrubbing toilets and battling alien goo. If anyone thinks space is glamorous, they clearly haven't seen the mess we space janitors deal with every single day. It's time they appreciate the unsung heroes of the space station ‚Äď the custodians of the cosmos.

/Locale/en-US/paper/book-escalation.ftlťéį        If deescalation doesn't work, it's time to grab a weapon. But be smart about it - choose something that looks like it couldn't possibly be deadly, like this book.

/Locale/en-US/paper/paper-misc.ftlťéįbook-text-agrichemkit-manual = Thank you for choosing the safe-for-all-ages NanoTrasen Agri-Chem is Fun! chemistry kit, the best learning-adjacent toy of 2468! /Locale/en-US/paper/paper-misc.ftlťéį      Contents:
/Locale/en-US/paper/paper-misc.ftlťéį      5 handily labeled 30u bottles, 1 each of nitrogen, phosphorus, hydrogen, potassium, and ethanol
/Locale/en-US/paper/paper-misc.ftlťéį      2 Safe-T-Fun(TM) sized 5u vials, 1 each of chlorine and radium
/Locale/en-US/paper/paper-misc.ftlťéį      1 30u empty bottle, ready for your experiments.
/Locale/en-US/paper/paper-misc.ftlťéį      First Experiment: EZ Nutrient
/Locale/en-US/paper/paper-misc.ftlťéį      To create EZ Nutrient, a fertilizer densely packed with nutrients required for healthy plants, mix equal parts nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium. To make a full bottle, thats 10u of each.
/Locale/en-US/paper/paper-misc.ftlťéį      A little bottle of EZ Nutrient replaces a big stack of produce you'd otherwise compost to keep your plants growing strong. And crops you compost are crops you didn't make a profit on, so keep that EZ Nutrient flowing.
/Locale/en-US/paper/paper-misc.ftlťéį      Second Experiment: Ammonia and Diethylamine
/Locale/en-US/paper/paper-misc.ftlťéį      Mix 3 parts hydrogen to 1 part nitrogen to make ammonia. It's a healthy, nutritious treat for your crops that aren't at their best. 
/Locale/en-US/paper/paper-misc.ftlťéį      But if you want to impress the judges at your local NanoTrasen-sponsored County Fair, you'll need to add an equal amount of ethanol to your ammonia.
/Locale/en-US/paper/paper-misc.ftlťéį      This will mix into Diethylamine, a miracle fertilizer that can make plants grow faster AND live longer! Keep the crops you love with you longer thanks to Diethylamine.
/Locale/en-US/paper/paper-misc.ftlťéį      Third Experiment: Unstable Mutagen
/Locale/en-US/paper/paper-misc.ftlťéį      When you're ready to show everyone you're the greatest botanist on the station, you'll need to mix up some unstable mutagen and hope for the best. Despite the scary name, unstable mutagen is entirely safe when applied to plants, but do not drink the appealingly green chemical yourself.
/Locale/en-US/paper/paper-misc.ftlťéį      
/Locale/en-US/paper/paper-misc.ftlťéį      Mix equal parts radium, phosphorus, and chlorine together to get a bright green batch of unstable mutagen, the safe and fun way to get the most of your farm. The 15u this kit lets you make could be your introduction to agrichemical stardom!
/Locale/en-US/paper/paper-misc.ftlťéį      Unstable mutagen can have a wide variety of effects on plant life, including drastic changes to all sorts of growth parameters, produce full of helpful pharmaceuticals, plants that glow in the dark, or creating entirely new species.
/Locale/en-US/paper/paper-misc.ftlťéį      
/Locale/en-US/paper/paper-misc.ftlťéį      Each individual plant responds to unstable mutagen differently, so you may want to use small doses on multiple crops and try to crossbreed the best traits from each of those. Applying multiple doses to one plant can stack multiple changes and make it harder to single out desirable traits.
/Locale/en-US/paper/paper-misc.ftlťéį      Unstable mutagen is entirely safe when used as a fertilizer, and NanoTrasen takes no responsibility for dead crops, excessive water bills, newly sentient plants asking existential questions, or flora-strangled farmhands that may coincidentally occur while using it.
/Locale/en-US/paper/paper-misc.ftlťéį      Do not drink unstable mutagen. Wash your hands thoroughly after handling. Wash your eyes if you have looked at unstable mutagen for over 30 minutes in a 24 hour period. Store in a dark room between 293‚Äď295K. Do not use on corporate holidays. /Locale/en-US/delivery/delivery-spam.ftlťéįdelivery-spam-13 =  paper
/Locale/en-US/delivery/delivery-spam.ftlťéį    .desc =         A letter to the editor.
/Locale/en-US/delivery/delivery-spam.ftlťéį    .content =      To whom it may concern,
/Locale/en-US/delivery/delivery-spam.ftlťéį                    The famous Adventures of Ian and Renault book series is one that is enjoyed by young children and at least one severely dyslexic lizard (don't worry, I got better). However, there was an error in the publication, as Ian and Renault would never make a mistake writing. It appears that in The Adventures of Ian and Renault: An Arctic Journey of Courage and Friendship, two lines from The Adventures of Ian and Renault: Exploring the City were used.
/Locale/en-US/delivery/delivery-spam.ftlťéį                    This made no sense. How did Ian and Renault find a little kitten in the Arctic? What was the little kitten doing there? Why were Ian and Renault doing more than average today? And the worst offender is the second misplaced line, which is "As the day turned to night, they were exhausted abut happy from their adventure in the city" (Ian and Renault, 1). OH, SORRY!!! I'M NOT AWARE THAT THERE'S A CITY IN THE ARCTIC!!! THAT THEY JUST STOPPED AT. YOU KNOW. FOR A COFFEE. OH WAIT!! COFFEE KILLS DOGS AND FOXES!!!!!!!!! WHO WOULD PUBLISH THIS!!!!! YOU'VE JUST KILLED IAN AND RENAULT!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!! HOW COULD YOU!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!! YOU MONSTER!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!

/Locale/en-US/nutrition/components/ingestion-system.ftlťéį## When trying to ingest without the required utensil... but you gotta hold it
/Locale/en-US/nutrition/components/ingestion-system.ftlťéįingestion-you-need-to-hold-utensil = You need to be holding {INDEFINITE($utensil)} {$utensil} to eat that!

/Locale/en-US/nutrition/components/ingestion-system.ftlťéįingestion-try-use-wrong-utensil = You can't {$verb} {THE($food)} with {INDEFINITE($utensil)} {$utensil}.
/Locale/en-US/nutrition/components/ingestion-system.ftlťéįingestion-remove-mask = You need to take off the {$entity} first.
/Locale/en-US/nutrition/components/ingestion-system.ftlťéį## Failed Ingestion
/Locale/en-US/nutrition/components/ingestion-system.ftlťéįingestion-you-cannot-ingest-any-more = You can't {$verb} any more!
/Locale/en-US/nutrition/components/ingestion-system.ftlťéįingestion-other-cannot-ingest-any-more = {CAPITALIZE(SUBJECT($target))} can't {$verb} any more!
/Locale/en-US/nutrition/components/ingestion-system.ftlťéįingestion-cant-digest = You can't digest {THE($entity)}!
/Locale/en-US/nutrition/components/ingestion-system.ftlťéįingestion-cant-digest-other = {CAPITALIZE(SUBJECT($target))} can't digest {THE($entity)}!
